title: Concurrent preference updates can silently drop data

evidence.quote:
function saveAll(data: Record<string, NotificationPrefs>) {
  fs.writeFileSync(PREFS_FILE, JSON.stringify(data, null, 2), "utf-8");

reasoning: POST performs an unguarded read-modify-write of a shared JSON file. If two requests load the same file concurrently and then each writes its modified copy, the later write can erase the earlier request's changes, including changes for a different wallet. Synchronous writes only block within one Node process; they do not provide cross-request or cross-process transaction safety.
reproduction: Send two POST requests at the same time for different wallets against a deployment with more than one request in flight. Both can read the same original notifications.json; whichever saveAll call runs last writes a copy containing only its own modification.
recommendation: Store preferences in a transactional datastore, or at minimum use an atomic update strategy with locking and write-through validation. Avoid whole-file read-modify-write for user preference state in a web route.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No linked tests are included, and there are no concurrency or multi-request persistence tests in the provided evidence.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add a test that issues overlapping POST updates for two wallets and asserts both records remain present after both requests complete.
minimumFixScope: Replace the file-backed store or wrap load/update/save in a process-safe lock with atomic file replacement and concurrency tests.
